It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, the player moved LEFT and the white strip with the blue center is now to the left of the left square. The left square seems to be back together. Let me check the segmentation.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions after LEFT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'c', 'W', 'G', 'b', 'g', 'P']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, after moving LEFT:
- Player: N at (24,28)-(26,31)
- White strip: W at (27,28)-(31,31) with blue center at (29,29)-(30,30)
- Left square: b at (28,32)-(31,39) with gray center at (29,32)-(30,38)
- Merged object: c at (24,44)-(31,47) with 2 blue centers
The white strip (with carried square) is now to the LEFT of the left square. The left square is at cols 32-39, and the white strip is at cols 28-31. They're adjacent.
So the white strip pushed through the left square and is now on the left side of it. The left square seems to have reformed (it's now a single object again, but shifted to the right).
Wait, the left square was originally at (28,28)-(31,39). Now it's at (28,32)-(31,39). So it shifted 4 columns to the right, and the white strip took its place at (27,28)-(31,31).
This is like the white strip "cut out" a 4-column section from the left square and replaced it with the carried square.
So the mechanic is: when the player moves with the carried square attached, the carried square pushes through objects, displacing them.
Now the white strip is at (27,28)-(31,31), the left square is at (28,32)-(31,39), and the merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47).
If I continue moving RIGHT, the white strip will push through the left square again, and then potentially through the merged object.
Maybe the goal is to push the carried square through all objects and place it in a specific position.
Let me try moving RIGHT to push the white strip back through the left square and towards the merged object.
Actually, wait. Let me think about this more carefully. The white strip is at (27,28)-(31,31), and the left square is at (28,32)-(31,39). If I move RIGHT, the white strip will push through the left square, displacing it to the left.
But I want to push the carried square towards the merged object, which is at (24,44)-(31,47). So I need to move RIGHT multiple times.
Let me try moving RIGHT 3 times to push the white strip through the left square and towards the merged object.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White strip at (27,28)-(31,31), left square at (28,32)-(31,39), merged object at (24,44)-(31,47)
# Move RIGHT to push white strip through left square towards merged object
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
